data.title: SS&C expects 2026 adjusted EPS of $6.93-$7.25 as it raises full-year revenue outlook to $6.672B-$6.832B
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: SS&C Technologies (SSNC) Q2 2026

Management View

  • "Q2 was record-setting for SS&C" (CEO Bill Stone), adding that the quarter set "quarterly records for second quarter in revenue, adjusted EBITDA, earnings per share and shares repurchased" and that results were supported by "new business wins, strength in multiyear renewals and market and transaction tailwinds" (CEO Stone).
  • "This quarter, we returned $499 million to shareholders, which includes 6.4 million shares repurchased for $435.2 million, the highest quarterly buyback in our history" (CEO Stone), and he said "share repurchases remain our top capital allocation priority" (CEO Stone).
  • "Calastone continues to perform ahead of expectations" (CEO Stone), and he described an expanded digital-investment roadmap: "The new capabilities will enable digital investment transactions to settle using regulated forms of digital cash, including stable coins and tokenized commercial bank deposits" (CEO Stone).
  • "The highly anticipated Medicare GLP-1 Bridge Program launched on July 1" (CEO Stone), and he said, "Since launch, nearly 3 million claims have been processed using SS&C's DomaniRx platform" (CEO Stone).
  • "Our second quarter results came in ahead of expectations with strong sales and renewal performance and continued margin expansion" (President & COO Rahul Kanwar), and he said SS&C is "deploying AI across our products and operations" with a "governance first" approach (President & COO Kanwar).
  • "Our Q2 2026 GAAP results reflect revenues of $1.696 billion, net income of $235 million and diluted earnings per share of $0.97" (CFO Brian Schell), and he added that adjusted results included "revenues of $1.697 billion" and "adjusted diluted EPS of $1.76" (CFO Schell).

Outlook

  • "For the third quarter of '26, we expect revenue to be in the range of $1.657 billion to $1.697 billion" (CFO Brian Schell), with "5% organic revenue growth at the midpoint" and "adjusted diluted EPS in the range of $1.73 to $1.79" (CFO Schell).
  • "For the full year 2026, we increased our expectations to revenue to be in the range of $6.672 billion to $6.832 billion" (CFO Schell) and "adjusted diluted EPS in the range of $6.93 to $7.25" (CFO Schell), while reiterating "EBITDA margin expansion of 50 basis points with the goal of a 40% margin in Q4" (CFO Schell).
  • Compared with the prior quarter’s call, Q2 guidance language added a more specific profitability milestone: "with the goal of a 40% margin in Q4" (CFO Schell), while management again emphasized "a stronger weighting to share repurchases versus debt reduction" (CFO Schell).

Financial Results

  • "Adjusted consolidated EBITDA, also a record, was $671 million" (CFO Brian Schell) with "a margin of 39.5%, a 50 basis point expansion" (CFO Schell).
  • "The adjusted revenue increase of $159 million was primarily driven by incremental revenue contributions from organic growth of $118 million, acquisitions of $36 million and a favorable impact from foreign exchange of approximately $5 million" (CFO Schell), and he said "core expenses increased 6.1% or $58 million" (CFO Schell).
  • "Cash flow from operating activities grew 11.1%, driven by growth in earnings" (CFO Schell), and SS&C ended Q2 with "$435 million in cash and cash equivalents and $7.6 billion in gross debt" (CFO Schell).
  • "Resulting net leverage ratio is 2.75x" (CFO Schell), and he said SS&C is "actively exploring refinancing opportunities for our bonds maturing in 2027" (CFO Schell).

Q&A

  • Daniel Perlin, RBC Capital Markets: asked why organic growth ran above guidance and whether license renewals were a factor; CEO Bill Stone: "we did have a couple of big renewals in Q2, and that's why organic revenue was up a couple of hundred extra basis points" and said ASC 606 can create "somewhat of a rush on revenue" depending on timing.
  • Daniel Perlin, RBC Capital Markets: asked how market activity may have influenced results; CEO Stone: said the hedge fund "redemption index was at 1.35%, which is about the lowest we've had" and added that assets under administration in fund administration were up "$581 billion" over the last two years.
  • Jeffrey Schmitt, William Blair: asked if AI is driving outsourcing demand; President & COO Rahul Kanwar: customers want to "take advantage of AI" and "Agentic capabilities," and SS&C can "deploy it in a very scalable way" (President & COO Kanwar).
  • Jeffrey Schmitt, William Blair: asked about GIDS growth excluding lift-outs and the lift-out pipeline; CEO Stone: said the pipeline is strong and that underlying growth "It's still in excess of 5%" while calling lift-outs "very accretive" but "competitive" (CEO Stone).
  • Kevin McVeigh, UBS: asked what the Medicare claims launch contributed and how it scales; CEO Stone: "We get in excess of double of the $0.20" and said it is "a 6-month program" with continuation "probably 50-50" (CEO Stone).
  • Isabella Camaj, JPMorgan: asked why Q3 organic growth implies a step down and whether upside renewals exist; CEO Stone: "Our pipeline is full of opportunities" and said, "we have opportunities to outperform" but "we're not going to pre-beat the drum" (CEO Stone).
  • Peter Heckmann, D.A. Davidson: asked about Intralinks’ sequential improvement; President & COO Kanwar: said pipeline, bookings, and conversion metrics "are positive" and added, "we are seeing the business strengthening" (President & COO Kanwar).
  • Michael Infante, Morgan Stanley: asked whether AI affects renewals as monetization upside or pricing pushback; CEO Stone: said SS&C’s approach is "showing people that our technology is better" and emphasized guardrails and orchestration to deliver "very sophisticated workflows that are not people intensive" (CEO Stone).

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were slightly positive, repeatedly calling out results and then probing sustainability and drivers, including organic-growth "lumpiness" from renewals, AI’s role in outsourcing demand, and durability of Intralinks improvement.
  • Management tone was positive in prepared remarks and remained confident in Q&A, using phrases such as "pipeline is full of opportunities" and "opportunities to outperform" while also showing caution on the Medicare program: continuation "probably 50-50" (CEO Bill Stone).
  • Versus the prior quarter, management’s tone shifted from emphasizing macro headwinds (Q1 referenced "war" and "tariffs") to emphasizing "record-setting" execution, while analysts’ focus shifted toward renewal timing, AI-driven demand, and specific program monetization.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q2 emphasized records and buybacks, with CEO Bill Stone highlighting "the highest quarterly buyback in our history," while Q1 emphasized macro disruption and said, "we are raising 2026 guidance" amid geopolitical and tariff concerns.
  • Q2 discussion elevated tokenization from preparedness (Q1) to roadmap and settlement mechanics, including "regulated forms of digital cash" (CEO Stone), and added operating detail on DomaniRx’s Medicare claims scale.
  • Analysts in Q2 concentrated on drivers of organic outperformance (renewal timing, market/transaction tailwinds) and the monetization posture around AI and Agentic automation; Q1 analysts more frequently pressed on macro sensitivity, segment risks, and capital return cadence.

Risks and Concerns

  • "It depends when these things hit" (CEO Bill Stone) described renewal-driven revenue timing as a source of quarterly variability due to ASC 606.
  • "It's a 6-month program" (CEO Stone) framed the Medicare GLP-1 Bridge Program as time-limited, and he said continuation is "probably 50-50" (CEO Stone).
  • "We are actively exploring refinancing opportunities for our bonds maturing in 2027" (CFO Brian Schell) highlighted upcoming debt maturities and ongoing capital-structure actions.

Final Takeaway

Management characterized Q2 as "record-setting" and tied outperformance to large renewals, organic growth, and margin expansion while reinforcing a capital-return stance centered on buybacks. Guidance was raised for full-year 2026, with targets that include a Q4 EBITDA margin goal of 40%, alongside continued investment in AI (including Agentic automation) and an expanded tokenization roadmap via Calastone, while flagging renewal timing variability and the uncertain renewal of the 6-month Medicare GLP-1 program.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Five Star Bancorp outlines Southern California expansion and plans another office later in 2026
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Five Star Bancorp (FSBC) Q2 2026

Management View

  • “Before we discuss the results of the second quarter of 2026, we wanted to note that, as you may be aware, we also announced yesterday the pricing of an underwritten public offering of shares of our common stock.” (President, CEO & Director James Beckwith)

  • “As the offering has not yet settled, today we will present only financial results for the second quarter of 2026 and will not be conducting a Q&A session today.” (President, CEO & Director Beckwith)

  • “Q2 2026 was another period of strong performance, reflecting the continued execution of our organic growth strategy and the strength of our relationship-driven model.” (President, CEO & Director Beckwith) “Net income increased to $19.4 million compared to $18.6 million in the first quarter of 2026.” (President, CEO & Director Beckwith)

  • “In April, we announced the expansion of our business in Southern California with the addition of 5 seasoned banking professionals to lead our efforts across the Greater Los Angeles area.” (President, CEO & Director Beckwith) “With plans to open an additional Southern California office later this year, we believe we are well positioned to deliver Five Star Bank’s high-touch concierge banking services to the clients and communities of Greater Los Angeles.” (President, CEO & Director Beckwith)

  • “We also continue to execute on our footprint expansion with the opening of our newest full-service branch in Lodi on July 13.” (President, CEO & Director Beckwith) “The Lodi branch further strengthens our food, agribusiness, and diversified industries vertical.” (President, CEO & Director Beckwith)

  • “Net interest income increased to $46.1 million in Q2 2026, a 6.04% increase from $43.5 million in Q1 2026.” (Executive VP & CFO Heather Luck) “Net interest margin contracted by 7 basis points quarter-over-quarter to 3.63%.” (Executive VP & CFO Luck)

Outlook

  • Management did not provide formal EPS or revenue guidance in the prepared remarks, and no analyst-estimate comparison is included because the call did not disclose guidance figures for EPS or revenues.

  • “Ongoing uncertainty surrounding geopolitical tensions, energy market instability, and uncertainty around the pace and direction of the Federal Reserve monetary policy have contributed to a more volatile interest rate environment.” (President, CEO & Director Beckwith) “We believe we are well positioned to navigate these conditions, as approximately 74% of our loans held for investment are adjustable or floating, providing meaningful flexibility to respond to market shifts.” (President, CEO & Director Beckwith)

  • Compared with the prior quarter’s earnings call, management’s updated prepared remarks emphasized the equity offering and the lack of Q&A: “we also announced yesterday the pricing of an underwritten public offering of shares of our common stock” and “will not be conducting a Q&A session today.” (President, CEO & Director Beckwith)

Financial Results

  • “Earnings per share rose to $0.91 per share, up $0.04 from Q1 2026, and up $0.23 from the second quarter of 2025.” (President, CEO & Director Beckwith) “Net interest margin of 3.63%, a decrease of 7 basis points from the prior quarter, and average cost of total deposits of 2.16%, an increase of 3 basis points from the prior quarter.” (President, CEO & Director Beckwith)

  • “Loans held for investment grew by $306.3 million, or 29% on an annualized basis, reaching $4.5 billion at June 30, 2026.” (President, CEO & Director Beckwith) “Total deposits grew by $330 million, or 30% on an annualized basis, with non-wholesale deposits up $463.1 million, more than offsetting the $133.1 million reduction in wholesale deposits.” (President, CEO & Director Beckwith)

  • “Ratio of non-performing loans increased to 0.30% at June 30, 2026, up from 0.07% at March 31, 2026.” (President, CEO & Director Beckwith) “This increase was attributable to one Community Reinvestment Act loan that was placed on non-accrual status during the period.” (President, CEO & Director Beckwith) “The balance of the loan is $11.4 million as of June 30, 2026.” (President, CEO & Director Beckwith)

  • “We recorded a $2.3 million provision for credit losses during the quarter, primarily related to loan growth.” (President, CEO & Director Beckwith) “Our allowance for credit losses totaled $47.3 million at June 30, 2026, up from $44.4 million at December 31, 2025.” (President, CEO & Director Beckwith)

  • “Non-interest expense increased by $2.2 million in Q2 2026 compared to Q1 2026, primarily due to a $1 million loss contingency release on an SBA loan that benefited Q1 and did not reoccur during Q2.” (Executive VP & CFO Luck) “Our efficiency ratio was 40.91% for Q2 2026 compared to 38.57% in Q1 2026.” (Executive VP & CFO Luck)

Q&A

  • No analyst Q&A session was included in this transcript: “we will not be conducting a Q&A session today.” (President, CEO & Director Beckwith)

Sentiment Analysis

  • With no Q&A, analyst tone was not observable in the current quarter prepared remarks; in the prior quarter, analysts pressed on growth, deposit mix, margins, and expenses, including questions like “Any updated expectations on pace of balance sheet growth or targets for the year?” (Andrew Terrell, Stephens Inc.: Question)

  • Management’s tone in prepared remarks was slightly positive and confident, emphasizing execution and positioning: “With the first half of 2026 behind us, we are encouraged by the results we have delivered and confident in our position rolling into the second half of the year.” (President, CEO & Director Beckwith)

  • Versus Q1 2026, management confidence language remained consistent, while Q2 added a more procedural note around the offering and format: “we also announced yesterday the pricing of an underwritten public offering” and “will not be conducting a Q&A session today.” (President, CEO & Director Beckwith)

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Q2 highlighted an equity-capital action and removed live Q&A, while Q1 ran a full analyst session: “we also announced yesterday the pricing of an underwritten public offering of shares of our common stock” and “will not be conducting a Q&A session today.” (President, CEO & Director Beckwith)

  • On balance-sheet mix, Q2 described a further shift toward core funding: “Non-wholesale deposit accounts constituted approximately 94.79% of total deposits at June 30, 2026, up from 91.43% at March 31, 2026.” (President, CEO & Director Beckwith)

  • On credit discussion, Q2 added specific NPL-driver detail not present in Q1 prepared remarks: “This increase was attributable to one Community Reinvestment Act loan that was placed on non-accrual status.” (President, CEO & Director Beckwith)

  • On growth strategy messaging, Q2 emphasized geographic execution steps already taken (Los Angeles build-out and Lodi opening): “addition of 5 seasoned banking professionals” and “opening of our newest full-service branch in Lodi on July 13.” (President, CEO & Director Beckwith)

Risks and Concerns

  • “Ongoing uncertainty surrounding geopolitical tensions, energy market instability, and uncertainty around the pace and direction of the Federal Reserve monetary policy have contributed to a more volatile interest rate environment.” (President, CEO & Director Beckwith) “Approximately 74% of our loans held for investment are adjustable or floating.” (President, CEO & Director Beckwith)

  • “Ratio of non-performing loans increased to 0.30% at June 30, 2026.” (President, CEO & Director Beckwith) “The loan is well collateralized, and a current appraised value provides significant cushion in excess of the carrying amount of the loan.” (President, CEO & Director Beckwith)

  • Funding-cost pressure was framed through deposit repricing and mix: “average cost of total deposits of 2.16%, an increase of 3 basis points from the prior quarter.” (President, CEO & Director Beckwith) “Growth in the average balance of non-interest-bearing deposits by $41.9 million helped partially offset the rise in deposit funding costs.” (Executive VP & CFO Luck)

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q2 2026 around continued organic balance-sheet growth, an underwritten common-stock offering that changed the call format, and ongoing California footprint expansion, while also acknowledging margin pressure from funding costs and a specific nonaccrual migration tied to a single CRA loan; as CEO James Beckwith put it, “we also announced yesterday the pricing of an underwritten public offering of shares of our common stock,” and “with plans to open an additional Southern California office later this year,” the bank said it remains “disciplined in our approach to growth and managing risk.” (President, CEO & Director Beckwith)

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Pool projects 2026 diluted EPS of $10.66 to $10.96 as inbound freight pressures gross margin
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Pool Corporation (POOL) Q2 2026

Management View

  • “Net sales grew 2% in the second quarter, a result that reflects the resilience of our model as strength in our recurring maintenance business and continued share gains in building materials offset a soft new construction and discretionary environment,” said John Watwood (President & CEO), adding, “we remain on track to achieve our adjusted earnings guidance range of $10.87 a share to $11.17 a share.”

  • Watwood framed priorities as “sales excellence,” “pricing and supply chain discipline,” “operational execution,” and “growth through disciplined M&A,” and said, “our fundamental strategy is unchanged,” while highlighting digital and product mix initiatives: “our proprietary and private label offerings continue to gain traction and POOL360 reached 18% of sales.”

  • “We delivered a solid second quarter, growing sales to $1.8 billion, holding expenses tight and generating strong earnings,” said Melanie M. Hart (Senior VP, CFO & Treasurer), while flagging margin pressure: “Gross profit grew 1% to $541 million with a gross margin of 29.7%, down 30 basis points year-over-year.”

Outlook

  • “Together, we expect low single-digit top line growth for the full year with approximately 2% to 3% from pricing,” Hart said, adding, “the benefit from pricing is expected to be lower in the second half of the year.”

  • Hart updated gross margin expectations: “Given the second quarter's weight in the year, we now expect full year gross margin approximately 30 basis points below prior year versus in line previously,” while maintaining underlying earnings expectations: “our underlying adjusted earnings guidance is unchanged at $10.87 to $11.17.”

  • On reported EPS guidance reflecting CEO transition costs, Hart said, “In the current quarter, we recognized $0.21 related to onetime CEO transition expenses, have updated our diluted EPS range to $10.66 to $10.96.”

Financial Results

  • Hart said Q2 net sales were $1.8 billion and “Net sales increased 2% over prior year, with a 3% contribution from pricing,” while noting category dynamics: “building materials volume grew again even with softer demand in other discretionary categories” and “a 2% decline in chemicals on lower pricing.”

  • On profitability, Hart said, “Adjusted operating income increased 1% to $276 million with an operating margin of 15.1%,” while “Reported operating income was $268 million, a decrease of 2% versus prior year,” and “Adjusted net income increased 1% to $196 million.”

  • Hart detailed a one-time item: “The adjusted figure excludes a one-time $8.3 million charge, primarily the noncash acceleration of unvested equity grants tied to the CEO transition,” and added, “As reported, earnings per diluted share was $5.17 in both periods.”

  • Balance sheet and capital allocation included “Inventory increased 4% to $1.4 billion,” “Total debt was $1.3 billion,” and “Our weighted average effective interest rate improved to 4.3% from 4.7% last year,” with shareholder returns of “$93 million in dividends” and “approximately $86 million in share repurchases year-to-date,” Hart said.

Q&A

  • David Manthey, Baird: asked where M&A is attractive and what operational fine-tuning is underway; John Watwood (President & CEO) answered, “tuck-ins, anything that's core to the business, we would be super interested in,” and on in-season changes said, “you can't really change too much in season. It's just all about execution right now.”

  • Manthey, Baird: pressed on investor skepticism around share gains; Watwood answered, “I think we've got a lot of right to win when it comes to building materials and it comes to chems,” and added, “I think our ability to go gain share is substantial.”

  • Susan Maklari, Goldman Sachs: asked what customers say is working/not working and how priorities stack up; Watwood said, “POOLCORP is really good at doing the hard stuff,” and on sequencing said, “it all starts when you sell something,” while on investment level added, “there's no significant major investments that really need to be made to execute on a lot of this.”

  • David S. MacGregor, Longbow: asked about leveraging technology investments and profitability of private label/Horizon/Europe; Watwood said “POOL360 adoption is 18%. That's a record,” and on Horizon added, “it was a tough quarter for them for sure,” plus “we brought in a new leader for Horizon as well.”

  • Ryan Merkel, William Blair: asked about monthly cadence and July, plus Horizon and negative trends in big states; Hart said, “there wasn't really anything significantly different,” and “July is pretty much on point,” while attributing state declines partly to Horizon: “roughly 1 point in each of those markets, was the drag that was created by Horizon.”

  • Ethan Roberts, Stephens: asked about runway for greenfield productivity and gross margin guide revision; Watwood said new sites have “a 1- to 2- to 3-year runway,” and Hart said inbound freight actions “will be starting early third quarter” but “we may not be able to recoup all of it immediately.”

  • W. Andrew Carter, Stifel: questioned gross margin assumptions and Pentair linkage; Hart responded, “There is no change in our guidance that is reflective of anything that Pentair has reported,” and reiterated drivers as “higher inbound freight cost and then also continued customer mix.”

  • Collin Verron, Deutsche Bank: asked what “sharply and competitively” means and the long-term growth algorithm; Watwood said it is “really understanding the market conditions,” and added, “under current market conditions, I don't think the 6% to 9% is on the table.”

  • Shaun Calnan, BofA: asked if large-customer mix is temporary and whether freight offsets are in pricing guidance; Hart said outbound surcharges are included, but “we don't have anything material included in the guidance at this time” for inbound, and Watwood said, “there's ways we can offset some of the margin pressures.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was slightly negative to pressing on gross margin durability and cost recapture, including questions around why second-half margin would hold and whether mix and freight are structural (e.g., Carter’s “that seems difficult” and Calnan’s “should we expect that to continue”).

  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more cautious in Q&A on near-term demand and margin, including Hart’s “we may not be able to recoup all of it immediately,” and Watwood’s framing of stability: “We all believe we're probably bouncing off the bottom right now.”

  • Versus Q1, management messaging shifted from stabilization and investment leverage under prior CEO to execution priorities under a new CEO, with Q2 introducing more explicit margin pressure language (Hart: “now expect full year gross margin approximately 30 basis points below prior year versus in line previously”).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Leadership changed, with Watwood opening Q2: “this is my first earnings call as POOLCORP's CEO,” replacing Peter Arvan’s Q1 prepared remarks.

  • Guidance framing changed: Q1 said “Gross margin for 2026 is expected to remain consistent with 2025,” while Q2 said “now expect full year gross margin approximately 30 basis points below prior year.”

  • Digital engagement accelerated, with Q2 “POOL360 reached 18% of sales” versus Q1 “POOL360 increased to 13% of net sales.”

  • Q1 highlighted broad-based category growth (including “Chemicals grew 8%”), while Q2 emphasized a softer discretionary and new construction environment and called out chemicals as down on price (Watwood: “chemicals declined 2% on pricing”).

Risks and Concerns

  • Watwood said “New pool construction stayed muted,” and noted “discretionary demand remained measured,” while Hart cited “higher inbound freight costs” and “unfavorable customer mix” as key gross margin pressures.

  • Horizon was a specific drag in Q2; Watwood said, “it was a tough quarter for them for sure,” and management said pressure was tied to residential/commercial project mix and timing.

  • On mitigation, Hart said pricing conversations are underway to “recoup the incremental cost” of freight, and Watwood said the company is focused on “speed, agility and flexibility” at the branch level.

Final Takeaway

Management described Q2 as a seasonally important quarter with 2% sales growth to $1.8 billion and maintained underlying adjusted EPS guidance of $10.87 to $11.17, while updating reported diluted EPS guidance to $10.66 to $10.96 to reflect $0.21 of one-time CEO transition costs. The central investment debate on the call centered on gross margin durability, with management attributing the guidance change primarily to higher inbound freight and large-customer mix and outlining pricing and supply chain actions beginning early Q3, alongside a strategy reset in tone under new CEO John Watwood focused on sales execution, pricing discipline, and leveraging the distribution network and digital adoption.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: AppFolio outlines 2026 revenue of $1.117B-$1.127B while raising non-GAAP operating margin to 26.5%-28.0%
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: AppFolio (APPF) Q2 2026

Management View

  • "AppFolio delivered a strong second quarter. Revenue reached $281 million, a 19% year-over-year increase. For the first time, we've crossed $1 billion in revenue on a trailing 12-month basis." (President, CEO & Chairman Shane Trigg)

  • "Units on platform grew to 9.6 million." (President, CEO & Chairman Trigg)

  • "To drive greater impact across their real estate operations, property managers are prioritizing technology consolidation." (President, CEO & Chairman Trigg)

  • "At the center of our system of action is Realm-X Flows, the orchestration layer that automates the work and governs how it gets done." (President, CEO & Chairman Trigg)

  • "We announced our newest agent, Accounting Performer, at Apartmentalize. It will accelerate the manual close cycle by streamlining bill entry, financial close processes and budgeting." (President, CEO & Chairman Trigg)

  • "We delivered a strong second quarter with continued revenue growth, margin expansion, healthy new business acquisition and durable customer retention." (CFO & Interim Principal Accounting Officer Timothy Eaton)

Outlook

  • "We are raising our full year revenue guidance to a range of $1.117 billion to $1.127 billion, lifting the midpoint growth rate to 18.0%, fueled by premium tier adoption, growth in new business units and increasing adoption of our products and services, including agentic AI performers and resident services." (CFO & Interim Principal Accounting Officer Eaton)

  • "Our outlook assumes a more moderate pace of unit expansion among existing customers, while new customer acquisition and retention remain healthy." (CFO & Interim Principal Accounting Officer Eaton)

  • "We are also raising our guidance for non-GAAP operating margin to a range of 26.5% to 28.0% compared to 24.7% in 2025." (CFO & Interim Principal Accounting Officer Eaton)

  • "We expect cost of revenue, exclusive of depreciation and amortization, to be relatively flat as a percentage of revenue compared to 2025." (CFO & Interim Principal Accounting Officer Eaton)

  • The analysts estimates JSON was not used because its fiscal quarter fields were not in the required Q1–Q4 format.

Financial Results

  • "In the second quarter, we delivered revenue of $281 million, growing 19% year-over-year compared to $236 million in Q2 of 2025." (CFO & Interim Principal Accounting Officer Eaton)

  • "Subscription services revenue grew 14% year-over-year to $60 million compared to $52.5 million in the second quarter of 2025." (CFO & Interim Principal Accounting Officer Eaton)

  • "Value-added services revenue grew 22% year-over-year to $219 million, led by our FolioGuard risk mitigation services, FolioScreen offerings and online payments." (CFO & Interim Principal Accounting Officer Eaton)

  • "We ended the quarter managing approximately 9.6 million units, an 8% increase from 8.9 million units a year ago. Customers grew to 22,751, up 6% from 21,403." (CFO & Interim Principal Accounting Officer Eaton)

  • "In the second quarter, GAAP operating income, which includes stock-based compensation expense, grew 31% year-over-year to $53 million or 18.8% of revenue." (CFO & Interim Principal Accounting Officer Eaton)

  • "Non-GAAP operating income grew 24% to $76 million or 27.1% of revenue." (CFO & Interim Principal Accounting Officer Eaton)

  • "We generated $88 million in operating cash flow during the quarter and ended the period with $222 million in cash, cash equivalents and current investment securities." (CFO & Interim Principal Accounting Officer Eaton)

Q&A

  • No analyst Q&A session was included in this transcript.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were not present in a Q&A format in this prepared-remarks transcript, so analyst tone and pressure points were not observable.

  • Management tone was positive and expansion-focused, anchored in adoption and platform breadth, including "AppFolio delivered a strong second quarter" and "this quarter, we crossed $1 billion in trailing 12-month revenue for the first time." (President, CEO & Chairman Trigg; CFO & Interim Principal Accounting Officer Eaton)

  • Versus last quarter’s prepared remarks, management kept an upbeat posture while shifting emphasis from early-year momentum to scaling adoption and raising full-year targets, including "We are raising our full year revenue guidance" and "We are also raising our guidance for non-GAAP operating margin." (CFO & Interim Principal Accounting Officer Eaton)

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance moved higher: in Q1, the company said, "We are raising our guidance for annual revenue to a range of $1.110 billion to $1.125 billion" and "expect to deliver between 26% and 28%" non-GAAP operating margin; in Q2, it said, "We are raising our full year revenue guidance to a range of $1.117 billion to $1.127 billion" and "raising our guidance for non-GAAP operating margin to a range of 26.5% to 28.0%." (CFO & Interim Principal Accounting Officer Eaton)

  • The product narrative in Q2 added a newly announced agent and more ecosystem connectivity: "we announced our newest agent, Accounting Performer" and "The Realm-X Claude Connector goes further, enabling true agent-to-agent communication with Claude." (President, CEO & Chairman Trigg)

  • Q1 highlighted a major internal leadership change, while Q2’s prepared remarks did not add a new management appointment; previously, "Kyle Triplett has been promoted to Chief Product Officer." (President, CEO & Director Trigg)

Risks and Concerns

  • The outlook embedded a growth-mix caution: "Our outlook assumes a more moderate pace of unit expansion among existing customers." (CFO & Interim Principal Accounting Officer Eaton)

  • Margin commentary pointed to cost pressures tied to mix and capacity needs: "Operating efficiencies were offset by our payments product mix and by incremental data center capacity to support rising customer usage of our AI capabilities." (CFO & Interim Principal Accounting Officer Eaton)

  • Seasonality and comparability were flagged: "We continue to expect 2026 subscription services and value-added services revenue seasonality to be broadly consistent with 2025" and "2025 included onetime revenue from a Stack partnership in the fourth quarter." (CFO & Interim Principal Accounting Officer Eaton)

Final Takeaway

Management described Q2 as a quarter of scaled growth and profitability, highlighted by $281 million of revenue, expanding operating margins, and $88 million of operating cash flow, while tying customer consolidation demand to deeper adoption of Realm-X Flows and Performers. The company raised full-year 2026 revenue to $1.117 billion-$1.127 billion and non-GAAP operating margin to 26.5%-28.0%, while noting the outlook assumes more moderate unit expansion among existing customers and pointing to payments mix and incremental data center capacity as offsets to operating efficiencies.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: EnerSys awarded revised $150M DoE grant for lithium cell manufacturing plant
data.description:
EnerSys (ENS) said post-market Thursday it was awarded a revised $150M grant from the U.S. Department of Energy for the development of its planned lithium cell manufacturing facility in South Carolina.

The company said it expects investment for the plant, which will be focused on the development and manufacturing of lithium cells for aerospace and defense and specialized industrial applications, will total ~$500M, with construction beginning in FY 2028.

The facility is expected to have an initial production capacity of ~1 GW hour to meet the specialized requirements of defense applications.

Given the facility's refined defense-focused scope, EnerSys (ENS) said it does not expect Verkor to serve as the project's technology partner going forward and instead plans to support the project by leveraging its established technology partnership within its existing aerospace and defense supply base.

EnerSys (ENS) was previously awarded a comprehensive incentive package through South Carolina and Greenville County totaling ~$200M.

data.title: Community Health Systems forecasts 2026 adjusted EBITDA of $1.3B-$1.375B amid softer elective surgeries and higher uninsured volumes
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Community Health Systems (CYH) Q2 2026

Management View

  • “Adjusted EBITDA was $330 million compared with $380 million in the prior year period on a 9.8% decline in net revenue, primarily reflecting a smaller prior period benefit from newly approved state-directed payment programs as well as divestitures completed over the past 12 months.” (President, CEO & Director Kevin Hammons)

  • “Same-store net revenue increased 2.4% over the prior year period. Same-store adjusted admissions increased 2.9%. However, approximately half of that volume growth was driven by uninsured visits with minimal related net revenue… resulting in a 0.5% decline in net revenue per adjusted admission for the quarter.” (President, CEO & Director Hammons)

  • “For the second quarter of 2026, financial results came in below our internal expectations… service and payer mix did not improve as expected, reflecting continued softness in elective procedures, along with higher uncompensated care, both of which drove lower margins.” (CFO & Executive VP Jason Johnson)

  • “In May, we completed a tender offer using proceeds from recent divestitures to repurchase approximately $368 million of the 4.75% Senior Secured Notes due 2031 and $231 million of the 10.875% Senior Secured Notes due 2032.” (CFO & Executive VP Johnson)

  • “During the quarter, we completed the previously announced divestiture of 4 hospitals in Arkansas for $110 million in cash and also completed the previously announced acquisitions of majority ownership percentages in Surgical Institute of Alabama in Birmingham and South Anchorage Surgery Center in Anchorage, Alaska.” (CFO & Executive VP Johnson)

Outlook

  • “As noted in last night's press release, we are updating our financial guidance for 2026. Specifically, we now expect net revenue to be $11.4 billion to $11.6 billion and adjusted EBITDA in a range of $1.3 billion to $1.375 billion.” (CFO & Executive VP Johnson)

  • “We continue to believe that the non-ACA-related payer mix and service mix challenges that we experienced in the first half reflect a temporary disruption in demand… However… we believe it is prudent to be more cautious about the second half of the year and therefore, adjusted our full year outlook accordingly.” (President, CEO & Director Hammons)

  • “At the low end of our guidance, we assume that the '26 year is not able to be recognized by year-end. At the high end, we assume that we are able to recognize the Florida 2026 and the benefit is consistent with the amount that we just recognized in the second quarter.” (CFO & Executive VP Johnson)

Financial Results

  • “Adjusted EBITDA for the second quarter was $330 million with a margin of 11.7% versus 12.1% in the prior year period.” (CFO & Executive VP Johnson)

  • “Results include approximately $40 million to $45 million in combined EBITDA contribution from the recently approved Florida and Indiana state directed payment programs… Of this amount, approximately $20 million to $25 million related to prior periods… offset by an approximate $15 million reduction in the Arizona state-directed program because of the prior period true-up.” (CFO & Executive VP Johnson)

  • “Same-store surgeries declined 0.1% with a notable decline of 3.8% in inpatient surgeries.” (CFO & Executive VP Johnson)

  • “Cash flows from operations were $87 million for the second quarter or $143 million when adjusted to exclude cash taxes paid out of divestiture proceeds.” (CFO & Executive VP Johnson)

  • “The company's leverage at quarter end was 6.7x versus 6.6x at year-end 2025.” (CFO & Executive VP Johnson)

Q&A

  • Michael Murray, RBC Capital Markets, Research Division: “What gives you confidence that the EPTC headwind doesn't worsen through the balance of the year…?” CFO Johnson: “So we estimate the quarter impact on the EBITDA front to be $20 million negative in the quarter… We do think the back half of the year looks like the second quarter… I feel comfortable with our increased range, which now sits between $50 million and $75 million of impact on an annual basis.”

  • Brian Tanquilut, Jefferies LLC, Research Division: “Could you help me bridge to that guide…?” CFO Johnson: “The miss in the first half of the year versus the expectations when we developed that guidance is between $60 million and $65 million… we took the second half down by $60 million to $70 million.”

  • Brian Tanquilut, Jefferies LLC, Research Division: “Curious how you're thinking about the drivers… on the cash flow side…?” President & CEO Hammons: “They're now auditing more claims before they pay them… So our AR is growing accordingly… we don't believe it's necessarily a collection issue. It's just a timing issue.”

  • Albert Rice, UBS Investment Bank, Research Division: “Can you break it down… and… any distinction… in the freestanding surgery centers…?” President & CEO Hammons: “Orthopedics being the largest decline… We are also seeing some softness in cardiac surgeries… we're seeing bigger declines in the inpatient side… our surgery centers are picking up, but it is kind of lower acuity surgeries.”

  • Albert Rice, UBS Investment Bank, Research Division: “Do you have any color on… admissions that are uninsured…?” President & CEO Hammons: “We were approximately 5% of our visits… prior year… and this year, we're about 110 basis points higher to just over 6% of visits.”

  • Jason Cassorla, Guggenheim Securities, LLC, Research Division: “Walk through… medical specialist fees…?” CFO Johnson: “The most significant component… anesthesia… when volumes are down… we have to pay the subsidy… the impact… was about $3 million of net revenue in the quarter… but it's still outpacing what we had expected.”

  • Jason Cassorla, Guggenheim Securities, LLC, Research Division: “Your thoughts around the proposed Medicare OPPS rule… 340B…?” President & CEO Hammons: “We think the net increase in outpatient rate for 2027 should be around 5%.”

  • Stephen Baxter, Wells Fargo Securities, LLC, Research Division: “More detail on the payer mix…?” President & CEO Hammons: “The increase in uninsured is primarily coming from the exchange business… There is also a decline in Medicaid… the softness in surgeries… is primarily commercially insured patients.”

  • Andrew Mok, Barclays Bank PLC, Research Division: “Can you elaborate… exit rate on surgeries…?” President & CEO Hammons: “June was our best month of the quarter… we were positive year-over-year in the month of June.”

  • John Ransom, Raymond James & Associates, Inc., Research Division: “Has the collectibility on self-pay deteriorated…?” President & CEO Hammons: “We only collect a few pennies on the dollar anyway… We're effectively not recognizing any revenue on that self-pay business.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • “What gives you confidence…” and “could you help me bridge…” reflected a slightly negative, probing analyst tone focused on the durability of ACA-related headwinds and the reduced full-year outlook. (Michael Murray, RBC; Brian Tanquilut, Jefferies)

  • Management’s prepared remarks were slightly positive on execution but cautious on demand and mix, including “financial results came in below our internal expectations” and “we believe it is prudent to be more cautious about the second half of the year.” (CFO & Executive VP Johnson; President, CEO & Director Hammons)

  • Compared with Q1, management language shifted from “Our financial guidance for 2026 remains unchanged” to “we are updating our financial guidance for 2026,” while analysts moved from early monitoring of EPTC/HIX impacts to more direct questions on the magnitude and persistence of the headwind. (CFO & Executive VP Johnson)

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q2 emphasized a reset in full-year expectations, with “we are updating our financial guidance for 2026,” versus Q1’s “Our financial guidance for 2026 remains unchanged.” (CFO & Executive VP Jason Johnson)

  • The operational narrative moved from Q1’s “Same-store surgeries declined 2.2%” and “ED visits were down 2.8%” to Q2’s “Same-store surgeries declined 0.1% with a notable decline of 3.8% in inpatient surgeries,” alongside heightened focus on uninsured volume growth. (CFO & Executive VP Johnson)

  • Strategic activity progressed from Q1’s “pending acquisition… Surgical Institute of Alabama” to Q2’s “completed… acquisitions… Surgical Institute of Alabama… and South Anchorage Surgery Center,” while divestiture actions continued with the Arkansas hospitals closing in Q2. (President, CEO & Director Kevin Hammons; CFO & Executive VP Johnson)

Risks and Concerns

  • “An unexpected increase in uninsured volumes and continued softness in demand for elective surgical procedures among commercially insured patients” was framed as a key near-term operational headwind. (President, CEO & Director Hammons)

  • “They're now auditing more claims before they pay them… So our AR is growing accordingly,” highlighting payer payment timing risk and rising accounts receivable days. (President, CEO & Director Hammons)

  • “Medical specialist fees… outpaced our forecast,” with “Anesthesiology and radiology” cited as “the largest pain points in this regard.” (CFO & Executive VP Johnson)

  • Uncertainty around Florida’s state-directed program timing was embedded in guidance assumptions: “the plan hasn't been submitted to CMS yet.” (CFO & Executive VP Johnson)

Final Takeaway

CHS management described Q2 results as below internal expectations as payer and service mix remained pressured by higher uninsured volumes and softness in elective procedures, prompting an updated 2026 outlook for $11.4B-$11.6B in net revenue and $1.3B-$1.375B in adjusted EBITDA; executives also highlighted ongoing cost controls, recent debt repurchases, and completed divestitures and ASC acquisitions, while acknowledging risks from ACA-related exchange disenrollment behavior, slower payer payments that are extending AR timing, and elevated specialist-fee pressure in anesthesiology and radiology.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Acme United outlines Med-Nap certification progress with goal to finish by year end as My Medic targets stronger profits beyond Q4
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Acme United Corporation (ACU) Q2 2026

Management View

  • “Acme United made progress during the second quarter of 2026,” said Walter Johnsen (Chairman of the Board & CEO), highlighting that “net sales increased from $54 million to $63 million, an increase of 16%,” while “net income increased from $4.8 million to $5.1 million and earnings per share increased 5% to $1.22.”

  • Johnsen framed integration of the January 2026 acquisition as the central execution item: “My Medic sales in the second quarter were approximately $4.3 million with break-even operations, as expected,” and management is working “to increase the core direct-to-consumer business as well as expand the product offering to retail,” while also “consolidating freight with other Acme United shipments to reduce costs, and eliminating duplicate corporate functions.” He added: “The intention is to have strong profits from My Medic during all quarters with particular strength in the fourth quarter.”

  • Paul Driscoll (VP, CFO, Secretary & Treasurer) said, “Acme’s net sales for the second quarter were $62.7 million compared to $54 million in 2025, an increase of 16%,” adding that “excluding My Medic, sales increased 8%,” and that “the higher SG&A was primarily due to the addition of the My Medic business.”

  • On supply assurance, Johnsen said, “When the war with Iran began, we placed orders for approximately $10 million of extra inventory to buffer potential product shortages and cost increases,” adding, “we continue to maintain this extra level of stock and are positioned to address issues should they arise.”

Outlook

  • Johnsen positioned the near-term setup as driven by mix, promotions, and easing tariff effects: “As we look to the coming quarters, we see continued growth of the first aid and medical business,” plus “resumption of promotional retail activity with our Westcott cutting tools,” “improving profitability at My Medic,” and “strengthening of our gross margins as high tariff products are replaced by lower cost ones.”

  • On timing of tariff-driven margin recovery, Johnsen said, “as we go through the rest of the year we will recover that 1%,” while also noting other pressures: “the freight has increased,” and “the dollar has weakened against the Chinese currency in the past year.”

  • Compared with Q1, management’s timeline language shifted from “a return to normal levels in the third quarter” (Q1) to “we expect the impact to lessen over the next two quarters” (Q2), per Driscoll’s prepared remarks.

Financial Results

  • Driscoll reported profitability and mix effects alongside higher operating spend: “The gross margin was 42.6% in the second quarter of 2026 compared to 41% in 2025,” while “SG&A expenses for the second quarter of 2026 were $19.9 million or 32% of sales compared with $15.8 million or 29% of sales for the same period of 2025.”

  • Driscoll cited the quarter’s earnings details: “Net income for the second quarter of 2026 was $5.1 million, or $1.22 per diluted share, compared to a net income of $4.8 million, or $1.16 per diluted share.” For year-to-date, he said, “net income for the first six months… was $6 million, or $1.46 per diluted share compared to $6.4 million, or $1.57 per diluted share.”

  • On leverage and cash generation, Driscoll said, “net debt increased… to $27.3 million at June 30th, 2026,” while also stating, “we generated approximately $15 million in free cash flow,” and reiterating acquisition and investment uses including “$14 million for the acquisition of the assets of My Medic” and “a cutting and sharpening line of products in Germany for $1.6 million.”

Q&A

  • Timothy Call, The Capital Management Corporation: Asked whether Spill Magic, Med-Nap throughput, and certification to expand sales to “government and hospital systems” are long-term initiatives; Chairman Johnsen: “Those are long-term initiatives,” adding, “the certification work is progressing well and we should be done with it by year end,” and noting My Medic’s performance: “My Medic business in general has grown about a third this year.”

  • Timothy Call, The Capital Management Corporation: Asked for mix of healthcare vs. cutting tools; Chairman Johnsen: “health care is about 70% of the revenues right now,” while adding on Westcott, “we’ve got a full book of promotional activity for back-to-school.”

  • Georgy Vashchenko, Freedom Broker, Research Division: Asked drivers of record gross margin and the tariff impact; Chairman Johnsen: “My Medic has bigger gross margins than our regular business, but they spend it… in advertising,” and said “margins this quarter were reduced by about 100 basis points due to tariffs,” while CFO Driscoll added, “it’s probably 150 basis points,” and “most of the increase in gross margin… is due to the mix of My Medic.”

  • Jim Marrone, Singular Research: Pressed on consumer appetite and back-to-school demand; Chairman Johnsen: “so far those sales are right on plan, and we’re not seeing weakness,” and on Westcott shipments, “through June, it’s a record for us, just flat out record.”

  • Jim Marrone, Singular Research: Asked what is driving product preference; Chairman Johnsen said Westcott has “utility patents” and “world-class pricing,” and in first aid, “we’ve got a strong sourcing team for components in Asia… and that’s why we win at places like Walmart and at Grainger and at Fastenal.”

  • Richard Dearnley, Longport Partners, L.P.: Questioned why My Medic is largely DTC and asked about seasonality; Chairman Johnsen: “it’s built half a million social media followers,” and “this is mostly direct-to-consumer today,” while CFO Driscoll added, “it’s probably like 35% of the sales of the fourth quarter of the year.”

  • Jake Patterson, Talanta Investment Group: Asked price vs. volume in Westcott; Chairman Johnsen: “Most of it was volume.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were slightly positive to positive, repeatedly praising results while probing margin drivers and demand durability, including “gross margin reached a record level” (Vashchenko) and concerns about “consumer… discretionary in their spending” (Marrone).

  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and remained steady in Q&A, using confident framing such as “we’re excited about the place we’re at now,” while acknowledging pressures with statements like “the individual consumer is being pressed, but we’re not seeing it yet.”

  • Versus Q1’s “difficult first quarter,” Q2 language shifted toward execution and recovery, with more emphasis on promotional normalization and integration benefits, while tariffs remained a recurring qualifier in both quarters.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q2 emphasized tangible recovery in profitability and promotions versus Q1’s tariff-driven earnings pressure, with Johnsen moving from “we expect to run through these items during the second quarter” (Q1) to describing an “improvement from the first quarter” and “continued gross margin expansion” (Q2).

  • Analyst focus broadened in Q2 toward consumer demand sensitivity, Canada softness, and gross margin composition, while Q1 questions leaned more heavily into Med-Nap remediation costs, CapEx, and the magnitude of tariff-driven margin impacts.

  • Management’s strategic through-line was consistent across both calls—retail expansion for My Medic, automation/capacity projects, and preparedness inventory—while Q2 added more specific commercialization timing, including “about to be going out to early distributors” for the next-generation compliance software.

Risks and Concerns

  • Driscoll cautioned about “continued high inflation, high interest rates, and the imposition of new tariffs or changes [to] existing tariff rates,” and noted that the company has “experienced supply chain disruptions.”

  • Johnsen pointed to cost and macro risks that could pressure margins, including “freight has increased” and currency headwinds, while using the $10 million incremental inventory as a mitigation: the company is “positioned to address issues should they arise.”

  • In Q&A, Marrone raised the risk of weakening consumer demand, while Johnsen responded that “we’re not seeing it yet,” citing robustness in Europe and strength in shipments and backlog.

Final Takeaway

Management portrayed Q2 as a step-up quarter with higher sales and improved earnings, while reiterating that My Medic’s high-margin model comes with elevated advertising spend and seasonality that concentrates sales in Q4. Across prepared remarks and Q&A, the company stressed ongoing integration actions (sourcing, freight consolidation, and eliminating duplicate functions), expected tariff-driven gross margin recovery over the rest of the year, and continued investment in capacity and compliance initiatives, including a stated goal to complete Med-Nap certification work by year end and expand My Medic’s reach into retail and institutional channels.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Market Technology acquisition prices $200M IPO
data.description: • Market Technology Acquisition Corp. (MTAK) said on Thursday it priced its initial public offering of 20M units at $10 apiece, raising $200M.

data.title: Galway Metals begins drilling at Estrades project under DOWA joint venture
data.description:
• Galway Metals (GAYMF) said on Thursday it started a 5,000-meter diamond drilling program at its Estrades project in Quebec under the Phase I work program of its option and joint venture agreement with DOWA Metals & Mining.
  • The company said two drill rigs have been mobilized for the program, which is funded by DOWA.

data.title: Albertsons signals fiscal 2026 adjusted EBITDA of $3.55B-$3.625B while targeting $200M ACI Edge run-rate benefits
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Albertsons Companies (ACI) Q1 fiscal 2026

Management View

  • "Identical sales declined 0.8%. Adjusted EBITDA was $1.013 billion, and adjusted earnings per share was $0.42 per share." (CEO & Director Susan Morris) "These results were below our expectations, and we're taking decisive action to improve future performance."
  • "Today, we're introducing the ACI Edge, a simpler, faster, more connected operating model." (CEO & Director Morris) "The ACI Edge starts with two decisive actions, moving from 11 divisions to 4 regions: California, West, South and East and centralizing center store merchandising."
  • "As the ACI Edge matures, we expect it to generate approximately $200 million of incremental annual run rate benefits with savings building through fiscal 2026 and the majority realized in fiscal 2027." (CEO & Director Morris) "We also expect approximately $50 million of transition costs over fiscal 2026 and '27."
  • "Including both our first-party and third-party businesses, e-commerce was profitable in the first quarter." (CEO & Director Morris) "Digital sales grew 13% this quarter with penetration increasing nearly to 10.5%."
  • "Our more cautious view reflects ongoing pressure on lower-income consumers, softness in grocery industry unit trends and the potential for additional affordability pressure from supplier cost increases." (President & CFO Sharon McCollam) "Our performance fell short of our expectations."

Outlook

  • "Identical sales are now expected to be in the range of negative 0.5% to negative 1.5% or 0% to 1%, excluding the 150 basis point expected full year headwind from the pharmacy IRA." (President & CFO McCollam)
  • "Adjusted EBITDA is now expected to be in the range of $3.55 billion to $3.625 billion." (President & CFO McCollam) "Adjusted EPS is now expected to be in the range of $1.75 to $1.85 per share, including approximately $600 million of share repurchases during fiscal '26."
  • "This assumes a gradual improvement in grocery IDs as our investments in the customer value proposition accelerate." (President & CFO McCollam) "The effective income tax rate is expected to be in the range of 24% to 25%."
  • "Capital expenditures are now expected to be in the range of $1.9 billion to $2 billion." (President & CFO McCollam)
  • The prior-quarter transcript was not provided with comparable guidance language, so changes versus last quarter could not be verified from the supplied materials.

Financial Results

  • "Identical sales decreased 0.8%." (President & CFO McCollam) "Reported results were pressured by approximately 100 basis points from the impact of the Inflation Reduction Act and 50 basis points from deflation. Excluding these headwinds, Identical sales increased approximately 0.7%."
  • "Gross margin in Q1 was 26.6%, a decline of 23 basis points year-over-year, excluding fuel and LIFO." (President & CFO McCollam) "The decrease in gross margin rate continues to be driven by the mix shift impact of outsized growth in digital sales."
  • "The selling and administrative expense rate, excluding the impact of fuel, increased 42 basis points year-over-year." (President & CFO McCollam) "In dollars, our adjusted SG&A was approximately flat, reflecting the benefits of our productivity initiatives."
  • "Q1 interest expense increased $25 million to $167 million compared to $142 million last year." (President & CFO McCollam)
  • "We then returned more than $300 million to shareholders, including approximately $225 million of share repurchases... and $84 million in dividends." (President & CFO McCollam) "Ended the quarter with a net debt to adjusted EBITDA ratio of 2.3x."

Q&A

  • Edward Kelly, Wells Fargo Securities, LLC: Asked for detail on "price investments" and whether the Board has considered "strategic alternatives"; CEO Morris: "we're investing very surgically and selectively... not about broad-based discounting" and on alternatives, "we're always making sure that we're considering every angle... but that's not the primary discussion that we're having today."
  • Mark Carden, UBS Investment Bank: Asked how centralizing center-store builds on prior initiatives and timing; CEO Morris: "we're expecting to be complete somewhere in spring, early summer of next year." Carden also asked about supplier increases; Morris: "As we look towards the second half of the year, we're expecting to see incremental pressure... our first response is always pushing back."
  • John Heinbockel, Guggenheim Securities: Asked about value perception variability and share; CEO Morris: "it's not a one-size-fits-all answer" and "we don't share specifically." Heinbockel also asked about exiting markets; Morris: "It's not necessarily about exiting markets... our primary focus right now is on our core business and driving an inflection in unit growth."
  • Thomas Palmer, JPMorgan: Asked how e-commerce profitability aligns with gross margin pressure and about ID trajectory; CFO McCollam: "e-com has tipped over into profitability" but "creates a negative mix shift" to gross margin, and on IDs, "very modest improvement... each quarter" with improvement "expected to be in the grocery side of the business."
  • Leah Jordan, Goldman Sachs: Asked about guide puts/takes and investment pace; CFO McCollam: "think about Q2 very similarly to Q1" and "when you get into Q3 and Q4... it's going to improve." Jordan also asked where investments go beyond price; CEO Morris emphasized "fresh execution" and "personalized value" alongside "Flash delivery."
  • Erica Eiler (for Rupesh Parikh), Oppenheimer: Asked how Albertsons differentiates beyond basics and when benefits return to "algo"; CEO Morris highlighted "micro market merchandising" and "macro resets"; CFO McCollam: "we expect it to start playing into 2027" and "we need to be looking for gradual and incremental improvement."
  • Paul Lejuez, Citigroup: Asked to quantify inflation and about unprofitable stores/real estate actions; CEO Morris: "cost increases... have been very moderate" and "we are not passing through the inflation that we're seeing," and on stores, "unprofitable stores is a very, very small number."
  • Simeon Gutman, Morgan Stanley: Asked about regional variability and whether the $200 million is "all going to price"; CEO Morris: "we do intend to reinvest that into price... and other elements of our value equation." Gutman asked if pricing strategy shifts to EDLP; Morris: "We are not making a huge change... It's really just about sharpening."
  • Robert Ohmes, BofA Securities: Asked about promotional environment and where low-income customers are going; CEO Morris: "a fairly consistent promotional environment" and "Our biggest leakage is to the big players so Walmart, Amazon."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts: slightly negative, with pointed framing around "tough" grocery conditions and a direct question on "strategic alternatives" (Edward Kelly, Wells Fargo Securities, LLC) alongside repeated probing on investments, margins, and competitive intensity.
  • Management: slightly negative in acknowledging near-term underperformance but confident in actions, including "These results were below our expectations" (CEO & Director Morris) and "our performance fell short of our expectations" (President & CFO McCollam), paired with commitment language such as "we're taking decisive action" (CEO & Director Morris).
  • Prior-quarter sentiment could not be compared because the previous-quarter transcript was not provided.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • The previous-quarter transcript was not available in the prompt, so guidance changes, metric deltas, and shifts in tone versus last quarter could not be sourced.
  • Within this quarter, management repeatedly linked a "softer unit environment" (President & CFO McCollam) to stepped-up investments and the ACI Edge restructuring, while analysts focused on the durability and payoff timing of those actions.

Risks and Concerns

  • "Ongoing pressure on lower-income consumers" and "softness in grocery industry unit trends" were cited as key demand risks. (President & CFO McCollam)
  • Management flagged "potential for additional affordability pressure from supplier cost increases" and indicated a mitigation approach of vendor negotiations: "our first response is always pushing back." (CEO & Director Morris)
  • Margin mix pressure from digital was reiterated: "the rate of the gross margin for e-commerce is much lower than traditional grocery." (President & CFO McCollam)

Final Takeaway

Albertsons’ management described Q1 as below expectations and responded with the ACI Edge reorganization—moving from 11 divisions to 4 regions and centralizing center-store merchandising—paired with “surgical” investments in value, fresh execution, personalization, and convenience. The company guided fiscal 2026 to lower identical sales expectations (with an IRA headwind framework), $3.55B-$3.625B in adjusted EBITDA and $1.75-$1.85 in adjusted EPS, while positioning ACI Edge to produce about $200M in incremental annual run-rate benefits (with $50M in transition costs) as savings build through fiscal 2026 and are largely realized in fiscal 2027.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
